head_coach.name: Mike Macdonald
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Defensive playcaller. Zone-based coverage with heavy pre-snap disguise and movement. Generates pressure via four-man rush, stunts, and schemed matchup isolation rather than blitzing. Offense: hired Shanahan-tree coordinators to run outside zone West Coast system.

offensive_coordinator.name: Brian Fleury
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Kyle Shanahan / West Coast (49ers TE coach & run game coordinator for 7 seasons)

defensive_coordinator.name: Aden Durde
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Dan Quinn (Falcons) / Dallas Cowboys DL coaching tree, integrated into Macdonald's Ravens 2.0 system

offense.base_personnel: 12 and 21 heavy; used heavier sub packages on 54.2% of plays in 2025 (6th highest in NFL), condensed formations at 2nd highest rate (54.2%)
offense.run_scheme: outside zone primary, supplemented by gap schemes, inside zone, pin-and-pull; fullback featured prominently
offense.pass_concepts: play action heavy off outside zone action, bootleg/rollout concepts, rhythm-based passing, quick game; ranked 1st in designed rush rate (51.8% early season), lowest pass play rate in NFL (51.3%)
offense.rpo_usage: low; this is a traditional under-center play-action system, not a shotgun RPO scheme
offense.te_usage: high usage; 12 personnel led NFL with 7.5 yards per play; TEs used both inline for run blocking and flexed out as receivers; Fleury's background as 49ers TE coach/run game coordinator will sustain or increase TE involvement; TE can function as move piece or X receiver per Kubiak's system
offense.rb_usage: committee approach (Walker/Charbonnet); backs valued for catching out of backfield, running screens, and zone fit; Kubiak explicitly emphasized utilizing RBs in passing game

defense.alignment: four-down nickel front; 87.2% nickel usage in 2025 (led NFL by ~10 percentage points); four-man rush on 79% of opponent dropbacks (3rd highest in NFL)
defense.edge_rush_source: 4-down DE hands-in-dirt and stand-up hybrid; primary edge rushers are Boye Mafe, Uchenna Nwosu, DeMarcus Lawrence, Derick Hall — mix of hand-down DEs and versatile rushers; 7 players with double-digit pressures
defense.coverage_base: zone heavy with pre-snap disguise; safeties rotate post-snap to mask coverages; Cover 3 base with match principles and situational Cover 2/quarters; man coverage used selectively
defense.blitz_tendency: light (19.0-21.8%); 2nd-lowest blitz rate in NFL; generates 5th-highest pressure rate with four-man rush (36.1%); when blitzing, 51.7% pressure rate (3rd in NFL)
defense.lb_role: ILBs primarily in coverage and run-fill; OLBs/edge players are primary pass rushers; Macdonald values positional versatility — defenders move between roles pre-snap
defense.safety_usage: extreme hybrid usage; Nick Emmanwori played 449 snaps as slot corner, 359 in box, 113 on line of scrimmage, only 7 as traditional free safety; Julian Love and Quandre Diggs serve as deep/intermediate coverage safeties; Macdonald deploys 3-safety packages frequently, as he did in Baltimore with Kyle Hamilton

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Seattle's Shanahan-style offense under Fleury (and previously Kubiak) is structurally TE-dependent. In 2025, the Seahawks led the NFL with 7.5 yards per play in 12 personnel and used heavier sub packages at the 6th-highest rate. Kubiak explicitly said TEs can 'split out wide, do X receiver type of things.' Fleury's entire coaching pedigree is coaching TEs (George Kittle) and coordinating the run game in SF's system where the TE is the offensive fulcrum. A receiving TE who can also block in the zone run game is maximized here — think Kittle's role. AJ Barner's physical play and receiving upside thrived in this system.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: While PFR lists Seattle as a 3-4, they functionally operate as a 4-down nickel front with hands-down edge rushers (Mafe, Nwosu, Lawrence, Hall). The OLB label is less relevant because edge rushers are deployed as four-down linemen, not stand-up OLBs in a traditional sense. However, Macdonald's system values positional versatility — Devon Witherspoon blitzed from CB, Emmanwori rushed from safety — meaning any LB with edge rush ability can be schemed into pass-rush roles. A hybrid LB/EDGE who can drop into coverage AND rush has moderate added value because of how Macdonald disguises pressure pre-snap.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: Kubiak explicitly stated: 'I've seen the guy catch the ball well out of the backfield which I think is really important for our backs that we utilize them... throwing him routes, throwing him screens.' The Shanahan system fundamentally values receiving RBs — from Marshall Faulk to Christian McCaffrey to Austin Ekeler. Fleury continues this philosophy directly from San Francisco. Seattle also used committee backs (Walker/Charbonnet), meaning a versatile back who can catch and block is essential to staying on the field. The play-action system creates high-value checkdown and flat routes for RBs.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Seattle is the NFL's premier destination for hybrid safety value. Nick Emmanwori's rookie usage is the blueprint: 449 snaps at slot corner, 359 in the box, 113 on the line of scrimmage, about 60 as an edge rusher, and only 7 as a traditional free safety. He recorded 2.5 sacks, 9 TFL, and 11 PBUs as a safety. Macdonald explicitly modeled this after Kyle Hamilton's role in Baltimore. He said Emmanwori 'can literally do anything' and wants to 'augment his role' further. Any safety prospect with size (6'2"+), elite athleticism, tackling, man coverage, and blitz ability gets a massive value boost for Seattle. This is the team that redefines what a safety can be in the modern NFL.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Seattle is the league's most scheme-distinctive team on both sides of the ball. On OFFENSE: Fleury continues the Shanahan/Kubiak system that demands TEs who can block in outside zone AND run routes from flexed alignments — a receiving TE is valued nearly as a WR3 here. RBs who catch are essential, not optional. The heavy 12/21 personnel usage means fewer WR3 snaps but more TE snaps than any typical offense. On DEFENSE: Macdonald's 4-down nickel with extreme disguise creates the NFL's highest value for hybrid safety/slot defenders. After Emmanwori's breakout, any safety with LB size, slot coverage ability, and pass-rush traits should be valued at a premium for SEA. Traditional box safeties or pure deep safeties get less of a boost — Macdonald wants swiss-army-knife athletes. Edge rushers don't need to be pure speed rushers; the system generates pressure via stunts and matchup isolation, so technique and motor matter as much as raw burst. ILBs must be coverage-capable — this defense lives in nickel with only 1 LB on the field most snaps.
